planting.note: Direct sow in fall in lean, very well-drained, sandy to rocky soil in full sun; antelope horns milkweed develops a deep taproot and strongly resents transplanting — select a permanent location and sow in place, allowing seeds to cold-stratify naturally over winter for spring germination.

directions: Sow direct in fall in lean, very well-drained, sandy, loamy, or gravelly soil in full sun; antelope horns milkweed develops a deep, fleshy taproot early in life and is extremely difficult to transplant successfully — choose a permanent site from the start and sow seeds in place, pressing them lightly into the soil surface. Asclepias asperula is a low, spreading native milkweed of the Southwest and Gulf Coast from Kansas and Oklahoma south through Texas and into New Mexico, growing in dry prairies, rocky hillsides, and open roadsides; it produces clusters of distinctive greenish-white flowers with purple to maroon markings from April through June, held at or just above the sprawling, rough-textured foliage — the curved, horn-like follicle pods that split to release silky-plumed seeds inspired the common name. It is one of the earliest-blooming milkweeds in its range and is a critical early-season host plant for monarch butterfly larvae and an important nectar source for migrating monarchs in spring; queen butterflies also use it as a larval host. Once established in appropriate lean, dry conditions, antelope horns milkweed is exceptionally drought-tolerant, long-lived, and essentially maintenance-free — avoid rich soils or regular irrigation, which will shorten its life.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry to average, well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; whorled milkweed spreads slowly by rhizomes to form small colonies and benefits from natural cold stratification over winter for reliable spring germination — do not amend soil or it will become lax and weedy.

directions: Sow direct in fall in dry to average, very well-drained, lean soil in full sun; whorled milkweed tolerates poor, sandy, rocky, or gravelly soils with ease and is well-adapted to the dry, open prairies, roadsides, and pastures of the central and eastern United States where it is native across a remarkably wide range from the Great Plains east to the Atlantic Coast. Asclepias verticillata is among the most distinctive of native milkweeds: its very fine, thread-like (linear) leaves are arranged in dense whorls of 3–6 around slender, upright stems, giving the plant a delicate, almost grass-like texture that is unlike any other milkweed species; in midsummer (July–August) it produces small clusters of tiny, greenish-white flowers with white corona hoods, modest in individual size but produced in great abundance and richly fragrant. It is an important host plant for monarch butterfly larvae and is also used by queen butterflies; because it emerges relatively late in spring, it should be marked to avoid accidental disturbance. Whorled milkweed spreads slowly by rhizomes to form modest colonies and self-sows in appropriate conditions; it is one of the most drought-tolerant milkweeds in North America and is an excellent choice for lean, dry native meadow plantings where the larger, showier milkweeds fail.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in lean, well-drained to dry, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; pink evening primrose spreads aggressively by rhizomes and self-sowing and is best suited to naturalized areas, roadsides, or wildflower meadows where spreading is acceptable — it can be difficult to control once established in enriched soils.

directions: Sow direct in fall in lean, well-drained to dry, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; pink evening primrose is exceptionally easy to establish from seed and requires no soil amendment — rich, fertile, or regularly irrigated soils encourage rank, weedy growth and even more aggressive spreading than usual. Oenothera speciosa is a low, spreading native perennial of the Gulf Coast, southern prairies, and open disturbed ground from Kansas south through Texas and into Mexico, widely naturalized across the Southeast and Mid-Atlantic; it spreads vigorously by shallow rhizomes and prolific self-seeding to form broad, low-growing colonies that in late spring (April–June) are covered in large, delicate, cup-shaped flowers of soft pink to nearly white with darker pink veining and a central yellow eye — misnamed 'evening primrose,' its flowers actually open in the morning and close in afternoon heat. It is visited by native bees, particularly specialist Oenothera-foraging sweat bees and long-tongued bees that collect its sticky, thread-connected pollen; it also supports the white-lined sphinx moth as a larval host. Plant in dry, lean settings and be prepared for it to spread — it is best treated as a naturalized groundcover rather than a border plant, and is spectacular when allowed to colonize dry roadsides, rocky slopes, and meadow edges.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in average to moist, moderately fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; sweet black-eyed Susan benefits from natural cold stratification over winter for reliable spring germination and blooms in its second year from seed, producing its best floral display from year 3 onward once established.

directions: Sow direct in fall in average to moderately moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; seeds cold-stratify naturally over winter and germinate in spring — sweet black-eyed Susan is more tolerant of clay and consistently moist soils than most Rudbeckia species, and performs best with reliable moisture during its establishment period. Rudbeckia subtomentosa is a tall, clump-forming native prairie perennial of the central United States producing softly hairy (subtomentosa = somewhat woolly) stems and large, three-lobed leaves with a distinctive pleasant anise or sweet fragrance when crushed — a unique trait among native coneflowers that gives this species its common name; from August through October it bears large numbers of bright yellow daisy-like flowers with long, reflexed ray petals surrounding a prominent dark brown to nearly black, dome-shaped central disk. It is an outstanding late-season pollinator plant for native bees, bumble bees, and migrating monarch butterflies; the persistent seed heads feed goldfinches and other seed-eating birds through fall and winter. Sweet black-eyed Susan is longer-lived and more robust than Rudbeckia hirta, forming steadily expanding clumps that can be divided every 3–4 years in spring; it is an excellent choice for the back of a native meadow planting, wet prairie restoration, or a tall native border where its height and late bloom season add value.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade to full sun; cutleaf coneflower benefits from cold stratification over winter and spreads vigorously by rhizomes — allow ample space and site in partial shade where other yellow-flowered natives fail.

directions: Sow direct in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; cutleaf coneflower germinates readily after natural cold stratification and establishes quickly, spreading by stout rhizomes to form large colonies over time — give it generous space and expect vigorous lateral spread, which is an asset in large naturalistic plantings but requires management in small gardens. Rudbeckia laciniata is one of the tallest native perennials in North America, reaching 4–9 feet on robust, branching stems bearing large, deeply cut (laciniata = deeply incised), pinnately divided leaves that give the plant a bold, tropical texture; from July through September it produces large numbers of striking daisy-like flowers with long, drooping, bright-yellow ray petals surrounding a distinctive rounded, yellow-green central disk — the green (not brown or black) cone is the most reliable identification feature distinguishing it from other coneflowers. It is one of very few tall, yellow-flowered native perennials that thrives in partial shade, making it invaluable for woodland edges, shaded stream banks, and partially shaded rain gardens where most coneflowers fail to perform; it is equally at home in full sun with consistent moisture. An important late-season pollen and nectar source for native bees and butterflies; the seed heads provide winter food for songbirds.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in lean, very well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; rough blazing star requires cold stratification for reliable germination and grows from a corm — established corms can also be planted in spring 2–3 inches deep; avoid rich or clay-heavy soils that cause crown rot.

directions: Sow direct in fall in lean, very well-drained, sandy, loamy, or rocky soil in full sun; rough blazing star is among the most drought-tolerant Liatris species, native to the dry prairies, open woodlands, and rocky glades of the central United States from North Dakota south to Texas and east to Ohio, where it thrives in the kind of lean, dry, well-drained soils where L. spicata fails. Liatris aspera grows from a rounded corm and produces a slender, rough-textured (aspera = rough) stem bearing alternate, linear to lance-shaped leaves and, from August through October, a spike of rounded, button-like, rosy-lavender to pink-purple flower heads with slightly fringed bracts — unlike the more densely spiked L. spicata and L. pycnostachya, the heads are spaced somewhat apart along the spike, giving it a looser, more open appearance; like all Liatris, flowers open from the top of the spike downward. It is an exceptional pollinator plant for migrating monarch butterflies in late summer and fall, and is also heavily visited by native bumblebees, sweat bees, skipper butterflies, and cloudless sulphur; the dried seed heads are eaten by goldfinches and American tree sparrows. Rough blazing star is an excellent choice for dry, lean, native meadow plantings, prairie restorations, and rock gardens where it out-competes weeds once established.
